# Reading Your First DDR {#sec-chapter-01}

## Learning objectives

By the end of this chapter, you will be able to:

- Explain why DDR PDFs are hard for software to read, and the difference
  between a *digital-native* PDF and a *scanned* one.
- Extract raw text from a digital-native PDF using Python.
- Write a script that processes a single DDR, or an entire folder of them,
  from the command line.
- Save extracted text so it's ready for the cleaning work in Chapter 2.

## Operational problem

Picture a folder on a shared drive: `Well_EXAMPLE-1H/DDRs/`. Inside it, 40
PDFs, one per day, named things like `DDR_017_EXAMPLE-1H.pdf`. Somewhere in
report 18 is the sentence that matters most this week: *"STK PIPE @ 9,960'.
WORKED PIPE, CIRC & PUMPED PILL."* Nobody remembers which report it's in.
Opening 40 PDFs by hand to find one line is exactly the kind of task a
short Python script should be doing for you.

That's the whole job of this chapter: given one PDF, get its text into a
Python string you can work with. Nothing clever yet — no search, no
abbreviation handling, no AI. Just a reliable way to turn a PDF into text.
Everything in this book is built on top of getting this first step right.

## Example DDR extract

::: {.callout-note title="Sample DDR excerpt (DDR_017_EXAMPLE-1H.pdf)"}
```
DAILY DRILLING REPORT
WELL: EXAMPLE-1H  DATE: 2025-03-14  REPORT NO: 17  DEPTH: 9,842 ft MD

24-HR OPERATIONS SUMMARY
0600-1130  POOH FR 9,842' TO CHANGE BHA. ERRATIC TORQUE OBSD LAST 3 STDS.
1130-1400  RIH W/ NEW BHA TO 9,700'. NO ISSUES.
1400-2400  DRLG AHEAD 9,700' TO 9,912'. ROP AVG 22 FT/HR.
```
:::

This is one of five synthetic DDRs you'll generate in a moment. They follow
the shorthand style real DDRs use — abbreviations, timestamps, depths — so
everything you build against them transfers directly to real reports.

## Theory

A PDF is not a text file wearing a costume. It's a page-description format:
instructions for *drawing* characters at specific coordinates, not a
stream of text with a beginning and an end. Two DDRs that look identical on
screen can be built completely differently under the hood:

- **Digital-native PDFs** were generated directly from software (Word, a
  drilling data system, a report generator). The text was placed on the
  page as actual character data, so a library can recover it.
- **Scanned PDFs** are photographs of paper reports. There is no character
  data at all — just a picture of a page. Extracting text from these
  requires optical character recognition (OCR), which we cover in
  Chapter 6.

This chapter deals only with digital-native PDFs — the more common case for
recent reports, and the right place to start. We'll use
[`pdfplumber`](https://github.com/jsvine/pdfplumber), a Python library that
reads a PDF's internal structure and hands back the text (and, later in
the book, tables and layout) on each page. There are other libraries that
do this — `PyPDF2`, `pymupdf` — and they're worth knowing about, but
`pdfplumber` is a good default: actively maintained, and its text
extraction is reliable on the kind of structured reports we're working
with.

## Implementation

### Step 1: get some DDRs to work with

Real DDRs are confidential, so this book uses synthetic ones that read
like the real thing. Generate a small sample archive:

```{python}
#| eval: false
# code/chapter_01/generate_sample_ddr.py
python code/chapter_01/generate_sample_ddr.py
```

This writes five PDFs into `datasets/sample_ddrs/` — reports 15, 16, 17,
and 18 for well EXAMPLE-1H (including the stuck pipe event on report 18),
and report 22 for well EXAMPLE-2H.

### Step 2: extract text from one PDF

Here is the core of `code/chapter_01/read_ddr.py`, built up piece by piece.

First, open a PDF and read one page:

```{python}
#| eval: false
import pdfplumber

with pdfplumber.open("datasets/sample_ddrs/DDR_017_EXAMPLE-1H.pdf") as pdf:
    first_page = pdf.pages[0]
    print(first_page.extract_text())
```

Run that and you'll see the report's text printed straight to your
terminal — depth, timestamps, operations summary, all of it as a plain
Python string.

Most DDRs are a single page, but some aren't. Loop over every page and
keep track of which page each chunk of text came from — you'll need that
later for citations:

```{python}
#| eval: false
from pathlib import Path
import pdfplumber

def extract_text(pdf_path: Path) -> str:
    pages_text = []
    with pdfplumber.open(pdf_path) as pdf:
        for page_number, page in enumerate(pdf.pages, start=1):
            text = page.extract_text() or ""
            pages_text.append(f"--- Page {page_number} ---\n{text}")
    return "\n\n".join(pages_text)
```

Note the `or ""`. If a page has no extractable text — a scanned page mixed
into an otherwise digital report, for example — `extract_text()` returns
`None`, and every downstream step in this book expects a string, not
`None`. This one line is a small example of a pattern you'll see
throughout the book: assume real data is messier than the happy path.

### Step 3: make it a script you can run from the command line

The full script, `code/chapter_01/read_ddr.py`, wraps this in a command-line
interface so you can point it at one file or a whole folder:

```bash
# One file, printed to the terminal
python code/chapter_01/read_ddr.py datasets/sample_ddrs/DDR_017_EXAMPLE-1H.pdf

# A whole folder, saved as .txt files
python code/chapter_01/read_ddr.py datasets/sample_ddrs/ --batch --out datasets/ddr_text
```

Try the first command now. You should see:

```
===== DDR_017_EXAMPLE-1H.pdf =====
--- Page 1 ---
DAILY DRILLING REPORT
WELL: EXAMPLE-1H DATE: 2025-03-14 REPORT NO: 17 DEPTH: 9,842 ft MD
24-HR OPERATIONS SUMMARY
0600-1130 POOH FR 9,842' TO CHANGE BHA. ERRATIC TORQUE OBSD LAST 3 STDS.
1130-1400 RIH W/ NEW BHA TO 9,700'. NO ISSUES.
1400-2400 DRLG AHEAD 9,700' TO 9,912'. ROP AVG 22 FT/HR.
```

That's it — you've just built a tool that reads DDRs faster than you can
open Adobe Acrobat.

## Practical exercise

**Try it yourself:** Run `read_ddr.py` in batch mode against
`datasets/sample_ddrs/`, saving the output to `datasets/ddr_text/`. Then
open `DDR_018_EXAMPLE-1H.txt` and confirm the stuck pipe event is in
there, word for word.

**You'll know it worked when:** you have five `.txt` files in
`datasets/ddr_text/`, one per PDF, and you can find the line
`STK PIPE @ 9,960'` in `DDR_018_EXAMPLE-1H.txt` without opening the
original PDF.

## Challenge exercise

**Challenge:** Extend the script to also print, for each PDF, the total
number of pages and the total character count extracted. Then generate
one more synthetic DDR yourself (add a new tuple to `SAMPLE_REPORTS` in
`generate_sample_ddr.py`) describing a lost-circulation event, and confirm
your script extracts it correctly. A reference solution is in
`code/chapter_01/challenge/` in the companion repository.

## Key takeaways

- A PDF's internal structure, not its on-screen appearance, determines
  whether text can be extracted directly. Digital-native PDFs can;
  scanned PDFs need OCR (Chapter 6).
- `pdfplumber` turns a PDF into plain Python strings, one page at a time.
- Handle `None` from `extract_text()` explicitly — real-world DDR archives
  will contain pages that don't extract cleanly, and your code should
  never crash on them.
- A five-line function (`extract_text`) plus a thin command-line wrapper
  is already a genuinely useful tool. You don't need machine learning to
  save an engineer an hour of searching PDFs by hand.

## Repository files

| File | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `code/chapter_01/generate_sample_ddr.py` | Creates the synthetic sample DDR archive |
| `code/chapter_01/read_ddr.py` | Extracts text from one PDF or a folder of PDFs |
| `datasets/sample_ddrs/` | Five synthetic DDR PDFs used throughout Part I |
| `notebooks/chapter_01_explore.ipynb` | Interactive notebook version of this chapter |

## Suggested next step

The text you just extracted is full of field shorthand — `POOH`, `BHA`,
`STK PIPE`, `WOC` — that means everything to a drilling engineer and
nothing to a keyword search or a language model. Chapter 2 builds an
abbreviation expansion engine so `POOH` becomes `pull out of hole`
everywhere it appears, turning this raw text into something both humans
and machines can search reliably.
